nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear-water coral reefs and rocky substrates at depths of approximately 0–30 m; larvae occasionally drift north as far as Massachusetts and Nova Scotia during summer

diet.notes: In the wild, this species has been documented consuming polychaete worms, fan and spaghetti worms, fish eggs, small crustaceans, and the polyps of hard and soft corals. In captivity it is often described as a finicky feeder, particularly on arrival; vitamin-enriched live brine shrimp, chopped clam, squid, and shrimp have been reported as useful starter foods, transitioning to mysis shrimp and frozen carnivore preparations once the fish is feeding. Small meals offered at least three times daily are generally recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke, 2914x1967. Tack-sharp, well-lit whole-body profile; white body, bright yellow fins, dark eye bar and the diagnostic dark spot at rear of dorsal all clearly shown.
Dan Schofield, 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ reef shot at a different angle; vibrant yellow fins, white body, dark eye bar and rear-dorsal spot visible.
Robin White, 1024x686. Shows a pair over the reef, representing this species' pair-forming behaviour; slightly soft but correct ID with white body and yellow fins.

summary: The Spotfin Butterflyfish is a peaceful Western Atlantic species recognized by its white body, yellow fins, and a dark spot near the rear of the dorsal fin. It is generally not considered reef-safe and tends to be a finicky feeder on arrival.
careNotes:
The Spotfin Butterflyfish (Chaetodon ocellatus), also encountered in the trade as the Spotfin Reef Butterfly, ranges through the Western Atlantic from Florida and the Gulf of Mexico south to Brazil, with juveniles occasionally drifting as far north as Massachusetts and Nova Scotia in late summer. It is among the more approachable Caribbean butterflyfishes for the hobby but is still generally regarded as intermediate-level due to its finicky feeding habits on arrival and its tendency to nip sessile invertebrates.

A mature, well-established aquarium with live rock, open swimming space, and hiding places between the rockwork is generally recommended before introduction. The species is most active during the day, tends to use all swim zones, and has been observed to develop dusky cross-bands at night. It has also been noted to swim inverted on occasion and has the unusual habit of squirting water above the surface in a fountain-like manner.

The species has been documented forming pairs during breeding in the wild, and aquarium pairs introduced simultaneously can usually be kept successfully; lone fish of the same species added separately have been reported to quarrel. It is not considered reef-safe — it has been observed to nip stony and soft coral polyps, tubeworms, and giant clams.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to form pairs during breeding in the wild and is oviparous. No reliable captive breeding has been documented for this species under typical hobby conditions.

additional.name: fishbase.mnhn.fr
additional.notes: Mirror returned HTTP 403; cross-checked the same species page via the fishbase.se mirror instead. Confirmed scientific name (Chaetodon ocellatus Bloch, 1787), family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes, maximum length 20 cm TL, depth range 0–30 m, Western Atlantic distribution (Florida to Brazil), temperature 23.9–28.2 °C (mean 27.2 °C), oviparous and pair-forming reproduction, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: FishBase (mirror swap)
additional.notes: Used in place of the mnhn.fr mirror after a 403 there. Same FishBase record; values quoted above.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: max length 20 cm, minimum tank volume 500 L, mostly peaceful temperament, not reef-safe (nips tubeworms, clams, soft/stony coral polyps), pairs possible if introduced together.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: temperature 23.3–25.6 °C (74–78 °F), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025 SG, adult 6–8 in, captive lifespan 5–7 years, minimum tank 100 US gal (~379 L), not reef-safe.

fish.breedingNotes: Forms pairs during breeding in the wild and is oviparous; captive reproduction has not been reliably documented under typical hobby conditions.
